coredata.dc:title: Blue-emitting fluorescent carbon quantum dots from waste biomass sources and their application in fluoride ion detection in water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Boruah, Anusuya
Saikia, Monikankana
Das, Tonkeswar
Goswamee, Rajib Lochan
Saikia, Binoy K.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are among the most feasible allotropes of carbon-based nanomaterials with unique characteristics of photoluminescence, bio-compatibility, and high stability. Herein, a green and eco-friendly approach has been propagated for the fabrication of CQDs from different biomass waste materials including sugarcane bagasse (SCB), garlic peels (GP), and taro peels (TP) by using ultrasonic-assisted wet-chemical-oxidation method. This top-down approach involves oxidation of the carbonized biomass wastes by H2O2. Another purpose of our work is to make a comparative study on the three CQDs produced from the three different biomass wastes. The properties of the fabricated CQDs were evaluated by using High Resolution-Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM), Fourier Transform-Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray Diffraction (XRD), and X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), respectively. The CQDs showed the characteristic photo-physical behaviours as evident from the UV–visible and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopic analyses. The CQDs are found to be highly water soluble possessing strong blue-fluorescence under UV light with excellent quantum yield around 4–27%. The comparative study on the different physico-chemical properties of the three wastes biomass-derived CQDs are also discussed in the paper. The FL properties of CQDs derived from taro peels waste shows the best fluorescence quantum yield among the three and keeping in view of this, an on−off−on fluorescence nanoprobe was designed by using taro peels-derived CQDs (i.e. T-CQDs) and Eu3+ ion. The FL emission of T-CQDs was observed to be significantly quenched by Eu3+ leading to the formation of a CQDs-Eu3+ nanoprobe. The CQDs-Eu3+ nanoprobe was promisingly used for sensing of fluoride ions in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent materials
Sugarcane bagasse
Taro peels
Garlic peels
Carbon quantum dots
Nanoprobe
Fluoride detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) from Biomass Samples
Fluoride Ion Detection in Water Solution
Physico-Chemical Properties of the Biomass Waste Samples
Morphological and Microscopic Study (FESEM and HRTEM)
Spectroscopic Properties of the CQDs (FT-IR, XPS, and XRD Study)
Observation from UV–Vis and Fluorescence (FL) Spectroscopy
Detection of Fluoride Ion in Aqueous Solution by Using CQDs-Eu3+ Nanoprobe

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of carbonized sugarcane bagasse waste and its EDS analysis shows the porosity and presence of smaller particles (<200 nm); (b) SEM image of carbonized taro peels waste and its EDS image shows the porous nature of the derived carbon material; (c) SEM image of carbonized garlic peels waste and its EDS analysis shows the irregular surface.
HR-TEM image of S-CQDs (a), T-CQDs (c), and G-CQDs; (e) shows presence of carbon quantum dots (<10 nm); and the corresponding particle size distribution analyses (b, d, and f), respectively.
FTIR spectra show the presence of –COOH functional groups if the derived CQDs (a) and XRD patterns of the synthesized three CQDs are showing their predominant amorphous nature (b).
High resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra of C1s and O1s of S-CQDs (a and b); C1s and O1s of T-CQDs (c and d); and C1sO1s of G-CQDs (e and f).

(a) Water-soluble CQDs under natural light (i) and under UV-light (ii); (b) UV–Visible spectra of the synthesized CQDs; FL emission spectra excited at 300 to 340 nm by an increment of 20 nm of S-CQDs (c), T-CQDs (d), and G-CQDs (e).
Steady state absorption spectra of T-CQDs and T-CQDs/Eu3+ nanoprobe.
Deconvoluted XPS spectrum of C1s in T-CQDs/Eu3+ without F− ions (a) and F1s of T-CQDs/Eu3+ with F− ions (b).

(a) SEM image of T-CQDs/Eu3+ without F− ions; (b) shows the element contents in graphical form (inset: tabular form); below images shows the elemental mapping corresponds to carbon(C), oxygen (O), europium (Eu), and nitrogen (N).

(a) SEM image of T-CQDs/Eu3+ with F− ions; (b) shows the elements contents in EDS analysis (inset: tabular form); below are the elemental mapping corresponds to carbon(C), oxygen(O), europium (Eu), and nitrogen(N).
Schematic presentation of the mechanism for CQDs and its interaction with Eu3+, CQDs-Eu3+ probe formation, and its interaction with F− ion.

(a) FL spectra of T-CQDs/Eu3+ with increasing fluoride ion concentration, (b) bar diagram showing the FL intensity against different concentration of fluoride ion in water, and (c) plot of FL intensity against different concentration of fluoride ion.
Types of fabrication processes and biomass sources along with their quantum yields.

row.entry: Sugarcane bagasse, garlic peels, and taro peels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physio-chemical characteristics of raw biomass samples (wt%).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: M-moisture; VM-volatile matter; Ash: ash content; C-carbon; H-hydrogen; N-nitrogen; FC-fixed carbon.

head.ce:title: Blue-emitting fluorescent carbon quantum dots from waste biomass sources and their application in fluoride ion detection in water

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Polymer Petroleum and Coal Chemistry Group
CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are among the most feasible allotropes of carbon-based nanomaterials with unique characteristics of photoluminescence, bio-compatibility, and high stability. Herein, a green and eco-friendly approach has been propagated for the fabrication of CQDs from different biomass waste materials including sugarcane bagasse (SCB), garlic peels (GP), and taro peels (TP) by using ultrasonic-assisted wet-chemical-oxidation method. This top-down approach involves oxidation of the carbonized biomass wastes by H2O2. Another purpose of our work is to make a comparative study on the three CQDs produced from the three different biomass wastes. The properties of the fabricated CQDs were evaluated by using High Resolution-Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM), Fourier Transform-Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray Diffraction (XRD), and X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), respectively. The CQDs showed the characteristic photo-physical behaviours as evident from the UV–visible and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopic analyses. The CQDs are found to be highly water soluble possessing strong blue-fluorescence under UV light with excellent quantum yield around 4–27%. The comparative study on the different physico-chemical properties of the three wastes biomass-derived CQDs are also discussed in the paper. The FL properties of CQDs derived from taro peels waste shows the best fluorescence quantum yield among the three and keeping in view of this, an on−off−on fluorescence nanoprobe was designed by using taro peels-derived CQDs (i.e. T-CQDs) and Eu3+ ion. The FL emission of T-CQDs was observed to be significantly quenched by Eu3+ leading to the formation of a CQDs-Eu3+ nanoprobe. The CQDs-Eu3+ nanoprobe was promisingly used for sensing of fluoride ions in water.

ce:section.ce:para: Conversion of biomass waste to create novel nanomaterials for various applications is a promising concept of ‘waste to wealth’ in the modern world of nanotechnology. Biomass wastes such as agricultural waste and domestic waste from various farming or food processing industrial processes are found to be the major pollutant in the land or water systems having severe consequences to the living beings [1,2]. With respect to this aspect of contamination, we propose a simple approach for value addition to different biomass wastes by transforming them into the carbon nanomaterials. Nevertheless, researchers have made several types of studies based on the fabrication of carbon nanomaterials from different biomass wastes (see Table 1 ). Sugarcane bagasse (SCB) is one of the major biomass wastes produced every year around the world. Globally, India ranks as the second largest producer of sugarcane after Brazil and it produces approximately 350 Mt. of sugarcane. About 40–50% bagasse (dry pulp and fibrous residue) is obtained from the sugarcane produced every year, causing severe land and water pollution which essentially demands the effective management of these solid wastes. Similarly, disposal of biomass wastes like taro peels and garlic peels generated in our localities in India, may lead to soil and water contamination, if not properly managed. Thus, a new approach for management of such industrial and domestic biomass wastes are urgently needed for their sustainable utilization.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the novel group of carbon nanoparticles having size less than 10 nm. It has drawn tremendous attention worldwide because of its exceptional properties of photoluminescence, great biocompatibility, high chemical stability, and less toxicity as compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots [3]. Because of its unique promising properties of fluorescence, they have huge applications in the field of bio-imaging, chemical sensing, catalysis, energy conversion, and optoelectronic devices [4–8]. There are mainly two synthetic processes for the fabrication of CQDs namely, “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches. In general, different carbon-based precursors such as graphite, activated carbon, carbon nanotube, coal, soot, carbonized waste carbon paper were used in the top-down synthesis of CQDs through different methods like laser ablation, arc discharge, electronic oxidation, ultrasonic treatment, and solvo-thermal treatment [9–12]. CQDs can also be synthesized by bottom-up methods from precursors such as amino acids, organic acid, saccharides, and natural products through microwave irradiation, thermal decomposition, hydrothermal treatment, plasma, and other drastic chemical template [13,14]. Table 1 gives us an idea of the different fabrication paths used for the synthesis of CQDs from various biomass sources. Carbonization of biomass proceeded through hydrothermal method is a green preparation method, but there is a major drawback of very low yield due to the excess amount of hydrochar produced as a by-product [15]. Also, most of the reported methods are harsh and time consuming as the oxidation involve chemicals like HNO3 and H2SO4 [16–22]. Our research group previously synthesized carbon quantum dots from subbituminous coals by using a wet-chemical ultrasonication method where H2O2 was used as an oxidizing agent [23]. In this paper, our aim is also to examine this established process for synthesis of CQDs from different biomass wastes.

It is to be mentioned that the CQDs and their composites are also used as fluorescence probes in wide range of applications based on detection of metal ions [24], organic molecules [25], and pollutants [2]. Dong et al. [26] used the photoluminescence (PL) characteristics of CQDs functionalized with poly-ammine for sensing of copper ions. Xu et al. [27] described the CQDs application in tartrazine detection in food stuff. Gogoi et al. [28] used CQDs for the recognition of various heavy metals present in the environmental contamination. Wang et al. [29] used the colorimetric paper-based smartphone for the detection of heavy metal ions in water samples. Chu et al. [30] used a smart phone sensing platform combined with a paper strip for detection of pesticides via a fluorescence “on-off-on” process. The aforesaid studies reveal that several research works were carried out for the detection of metal ions by CQDs. Fluoride ion, a non-metallic ion with various physiological and chemical properties is regarded as a major contaminant present in ground water due to its high toxic nature [31]. Fluoride ion can also be linked up with “nerve agents” deteriorating the nervous system of human beings and this ion is also involved in most uranium enrichment methods [31,32]. Thus, the fluoride ion detection has become an active field of research as it is highly toxic and widely present in drinking water in different parts of the world. Though several commercial detection methods like UV (colorimetric method) [33,34], NMR spectroscopy [35], and ion-selective electrode [36] were widely used for this purpose, they have many limitations like trained workforce, fully-equipped laboratory, and complex instrumentation. So, fluorescence-based sensing methods have become an alternative to overcome these complex detection methods [37,38]. Over the years, however researchers have made many studies on the methods for detection of fluoride ion. For example, Liu et al. [39] designed zirconium-bound complex CQDs for F− ion detection which required high temperature. Butler [40] analyzed a synthetic process by fabricating two dissimilar Eu3+ complexes for detection of fluoride ion but the process was seemed to be tedious. Singh et al. [41] developed an on-off-on probe which is based on Eu3+ coupled with CdTe-QDs for F− detection. But the toxic nature of cadmium precludes these quantum dots to be applicable for real use. Singhal et al. [24] generated a CQDs-Eu3+ nanoprobe, comprising pure citric acid as the precursor material for CQDs for F− sensing. However, from environmental and waste management perspectives, we have developed an efficient fluoride sensing probe by using CQDs derived from taro peels waste with promising quantum yield. Our process has novelty as it overcomes the drawbacks like time consumption and bio-compatibility. As CQDs are derived from biomass waste, our process does not involve any harsh precursor or pure chemicals, so the fluoride sensing probe fabricated is purely environmental friendly. As Eu3+ is a hard acid having a great affinity for a hard base like F− ion, CQDs-Eu3+ coupling is considered to be as a suitable probe for the detection of fluoride ion.

The present work also emphasizes on a comparative study on the different CQDs produced from the three different types of biomass wastes through the ultrasonic assisted wet-chemical oxidation method. The surface morphology and chemical characteristics of the different CQDs were studied including their photo-physical properties. We fabricated a probe with high quantum yield CQDs derived from taro peels and Eu3+for using as a sensor to detect F− ion in water.

About 1 kg of each sugarcane bagasse (SCB), taro peels (TP), garlic peels (GP) waste were obtained from the local/domestic areas of the Jorhat district, Assam (India). All the samples were air and sun-dried up to seven days and cut into small pieces of about 1-mm sizes. The samples were sieved in a mesh size of about 0.211 mm. The samples were then carbonized in a muffle furnace at about 300 °C in inert atmosphere. H2O2 (Merck; 30%) was used for oxidation of the carbonized samples. Europium (III) nitrate pentahydrate and sodium fluoride were bought from Sigma Aldrich.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) from Biomass Samples
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g of each of carbonized SCB, GP, and TP samples were dissolved in 100 mL of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as a solvent in a beaker. H2O2 was added slowly to the carbonized biomass samples in the beaker. The reaction mixture was then ultrasonicated (frequency: ~40 kHz, output power ~ 700 W) in a microprocessor-based sonicator for 3–4 h at an atmospheric pressure and temperature. It was then allowed to cool down naturally and ammonium solution was added to neutralize the mixture. The mixture was then filtered through 0.22-μm filter paper and purified by using the dialysis Millipore kit (Sigma Aldrich) of 1 kDa. The purified solution was then concentrated to attain the required CQDs. The obtained CQDs are coded as S-CQDs (derived from sugarcane baggase), G-CQDs (derived from garlic peels), and T-CQDs (derived from taro peels), respectively in the subsequent sections.

Thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA-701 Leco) and Truspec CHN (Macro Determinator Leco) were used for proximate and ultimate analysis of the samples. The surface morphology of the carbonized biomass samples were analyzed by using the field emission-scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (Model: ZEISS, Sigma) with the help of an auto fine coater. HRTEM analysis was carried out under High Resolution-Transmittance Electron Microscope (Model: JEM-2100 PlusElectron Microscope). TEM samples were prepared on a carbon-coated copper grid by adding two drops from the CQDs solution and then allowing it to dry overnight at room temperature. The SEM and HR-TEM images were further resolved by using “Image J" program (software version 1.47). The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by using a Rigaku ULTIMA IV diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) as an X-ray source. The FT-IR spectra were recorded by using Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (SPECTRUM TWO). The XPS spectra of the obtained CQDs were recorded in X-Ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (model: ESCALAB Xi+). The UV–Vis (Ultraviolet-visible) spectra were recorded with a UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (UV 1000, LabIndia) and the Fluorescence spectra were recorded in a Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba; Fluorlolog-3).

The fluorescence (FL) quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized CQDs was measured by using the following equation and methods elsewhere [23]:
Φ =
Φ r
× I / Ir ×
A r
/ A × η /
η r

Where “Φ” denoted the relative quantum yields of CQDs with respect to the quantum yield of quinine sulphate (0.1 mg/L) reference (Φr). “I” denoted the measured integrated FL intensity of CQDs, optical density i.e. absorbance is denoted by “A”, and “η” stands for the refractive index of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluoride Ion Detection in Water Solution
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized T-CQDs were satisfactorily used for detection of fluoride ion in different standard aqueous solutions of NaF. For this study, we prepared a standard solution of the as-synthesized T-CQDs (concentration: 19.98 mg/mL) and used this to measure the entire fluorescence emission spectra of the samples. Eu3+ (100 mg/L) was added to this T-CQDs solution in 1:1 ratio, which quenched the FL intensity of the T-CQDs. The oxygen containing functional groups present in the CQDs generally form a complex with Eu3+ leading to the formation of a CQDs-Eu3+ nanoprobe. Now aqueous solutions with different fluoride ion concentrations (0–160 ppm) were added to the nanoprobe and the regeneration of FL emission spectra was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physico-Chemical Properties of the Biomass Waste Samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proximate and ultimate analysis of the biomass waste samples is summarized in Table 2 . It is observed that the raw biomass samples have different compositions of carbon (30–50%), hydrogen (5–7%), nitrogen (13–80%), volatile matters (60–80%), and ash contents (2–11%). It is to be noted that the highest percentage of carbon and volatile matter are found to be present in sugarcane bagasse; and garlic peels have the highest nitrogen content than the rest two samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and Microscopic Study (FESEM and HRTEM)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a-c represents the FE-SEM analysis of carbonized sugarcane bagasse, taro peel waste, and garlic peel waste, respectively. The smaller segregated particles with uneven and rough surfaces are observed in the SEM images of carbonized sugar bagasse (see Fig. 1a) and taro peel waste (see Fig. 1b). The SEM image of carbonized garlic peels waste appears to have porous structure with irregular surface morphology as depicted in Fig. 1c. From the corresponding SEM-EDS images (Fig. 1a-c, right side) of the carbonized biomass samples, it is revealed that they mainly consist of elements like C, O, N, etc. Other elements like K, Ca, Mg, Al, Si, and Fe are also present in the carbonized biomass samples as indicated by the EDS image analysis.

Fig. 2a,c,e shows the TEM images of sugarcane bagasse-derived carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs), taro peel waste-derived carbon quantum dots (T-CQDs), and garlic peel waste-derived carbon quantum dots (G-CQDs), respectively. The corresponding size distribution images are also depicted in Fig. 2b,d,f. From the TEM images, the microstructure of the synthesized carbon quantum dots at the nano-level is clearly observed. The diameter of the synthesized CQDs lies in the range of 8–12 nm for S-CQDs (Fig. 2b), 0–2 nm and 8–12 nm for T-CQDs (Fig. 2d), and 8–11 nm for G-CQDs (Fig. 2f). From the size distribution analysis, we observed that T-CQDs consist of carbon quantum dots with lower sizes of 0–2 nm than the other two CQDs synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic Properties of the CQDs (FT-IR, XPS, and XRD Study)
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR analysis of CQDs derived from the different carbonized biomass samples are shown in the Fig. 3a. The strong band observed at 3300 cm−1 denotes the carboxylic acid OH functional group present in the CQDs. Also, the strong band observed at 1650 cm−1 denotes the CO functional group and at 1680 cm−1 denotes the presence of CC functional group. The band appeared at 2850 cm−1 represents the alkane CH functional groups. Similar results were also reported by other researchers [4,23]. From the Fig. 3(a), we found that G-CQDs shows the maximum intense transmittance peak of CO and CC as compared to the others. Also the strong band at 3300 cm−1 for the OH functional group is observed more clearly in S-CQDs and T-CQDs than the rest one.

The XRD analysis as shown in Fig. 3b, the diffraction peaks observed at 24.3, 23.6, and 23.9 for G-CQDs, T-CQDs, and S-CQDs, respectively clearly indicated the formation of carbon nanoparticles [4,24]. The wide peak observed at 23.6 for the sample T-CQDs is mainly due to graphitic carbon present [4].

The XPS spectroscopic analyses of the produced three CQDs were evaluated (see Fig. 4 ). The high-resolution C1s de-convoluted XPS spectra show peaks at 283.80 eV (C-C/C=C) and 288.30 eV (O-C=O) for S-CQDs (Fig. 4a); 284.6 eV (C-C/C=C) and 287.60 eV (O-C=O) for T-CQDs (Fig. 4c); and 284.2 eV (CC) and 287.70 eV (O-C=O) for G-CQDs (Fig. 4e), respectively [42]. Similarly, the high-resolution O1s deconvoluted XPS spectra peaks of S-CQDs (Fig. 4b), T-CQDs (Fig. 4d), and G-CQDs (Fig. 4f) are observed at 531.2 eV, 531.8, and 531.6 eV, respectively [43].

From both the FT-IR and XPS analyses, it could be summarized that the carbonized biomass waste-derived carbon quantum dots contain abundant oxygen-containing functional groups with sp2 aromatic structure units. The high oxygen-containing functional groups of the synthesized CQDs impart high solubility in water making it more advantageous for further application in fluoride ion detection in aqueous media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation from UV–Vis and Fluorescence (FL) Spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Visible and FL spectroscopy were used to determine the photo-physical properties of the synthesized CQDs from three biomass waste. The biomass waste derived CQDs appear to be blue-fluorescent under UV-light at 365 nm as depicted in Fig. 5a (ii). The UV–visible spectra of the biomass waste derived CQDs is also depicted in Fig. 5b and the UV bands appeared at around 260–280 nm are due to the excitation of electrons from π to π٭ of the conjugated system containing π-electrons. Similar results were also observed by other researchers [23,44].

Fig. 5c-e shows the FL emission spectra of the three biomass waste derived CQDs, which are excitation dependent. The FL emission spectra mainly depend on the wavelength at which it is excited and there is a bathochromic shift as we increase the excitation of longer wavelength [45,46]. The maximum intensity of FL emission wavelength is found to be in the blue-regions (460 nm). Owing to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups such as OH, CO and auxochromes like NH2, the synthesized CQDs show fluorescence property. Also the reason behind the fluorescence behavior of CQDs is also because of the different sizes and surface states of nanoscale carbon materials [26]. From the observed photo-physical properties of the synthesized CQDs, it is could be concluded that the synthesized CQDs contains conjugated π-bonds and optically sensitive defect points which make it promising carbon nanomaterials for high performance applications, most importantly in the field of chemical sensing, bio imaging, and other bio-medical applications.

The quantum yields of the synthesized CQDs are found to be 4.45% (S-CQDs), 26.20% (T-CQDs), and 13.80% (G-CQDs), respectively. The obtained quantum yield is found to be comparable and greater than the reported values of other biomass materials elsewhere (see Table 1). From our comparative study, it is evident that T-CQDs derived from taro peels show the highest fluorescence quantum yield of 26.20%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fluoride Ion in Aqueous Solution by Using CQDs-Eu3+ Nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The taro peels-derived CQDs (i.e. T-CQDs) was used for fluoride ion detection through fabrication of CQDs-Eu3+ nanoprobe. Due to the presence of oxygen containing functional groups like carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs, it is found to be a promising detector for the determination of fluoride ions. We studied the photo-chemical reactions between T-CQDs and Eu3+ by exciting the samples at 320 nm and observed the fluorescence quenching of the emission spectra when mixed with Eu3+ of 100 ppm concentration. The change is shown in the FL spectra in Fig. 11 (a). It is to be mentioned that the CQDs have sufficient electron donor groups that can be utilized in various applications which is also reported by earlier studies. [25,47,48]. For example, Zhou et al. [2] utilized CQDs for detection of Hg2+ ion, revealing that the transfer of electrons take place from CQDs to mercury ion (Hg2+) causing the quenching of luminescence intensity of CQDs. Similarly, Singh et al. [41] also detected the quenching of CdTe-QDs by the addition of Eu3+. Also Das et al. [47] utilized CQDs for removal of organic pollutants mentioning the transfer of electrons between CQDs and pollutants. However, in our study, we carried out steady state absorption studies of the coupled system of T-CQDs and Eu3+ (see Fig. 6 ). This suggests that there is no complex formation between T-CQDs and Eu3+ because there is no observation on shifting of the absorption peak. We only observed that the absorption peak becomes less confined in comparison to the absorption peak of T-CQDs suggesting the binding of Eu3+ with the carboxylic groups present on the surface of T-CQDs. This is also examined by the XPS studies (see Fig. 7a), which reveals that there is a binding of Eu3+ with the -COOH group present on the surface of CQDs leading to a faster fluorescence quenching. Also, we observed some micro-rods like structures in the FESEM image analysis revealing the formation of T-CQDs/Eu3+nanoprobe (Fig. 8a) due to the result of the interaction between T-CQDs and Eu3+. Almost similar mechanisms were also reported elsewhere [49]. The coupling interaction between T-CQDs and Eu3+ is also verified from EDS and elemental mapping analysis (Fig. 8). Thus, the plausible mechanism of the process is depicted in the schematic representation as shown in Fig. 10.

It is also to be mentioned that the Eu3+ is a hard acid and it will have a great affinity for hard base like fluoride ion in water. Subsequently on adding fluoride ions of different concentrations to this T-CQDs/Eu3+ nanoprobe (1:1) system, we again clearly observed the regeneration of FL emission spectra of T-CQDs. (Fig. 11a). This was also verified from the FE-SEM image analysis of T-CQDs/Eu3+ with 100 ppm F− solution. The micro-rods like structure produced as a result of the interaction of T-CQDs and Eu3+ appeared to be disintegrated after adding 100 ppm F− (see Fig. 9a). Also, from the atomic analysis in EDS (Fig. 9b) and elemental mapping study of FE-SEM images, it is observed that the percentage of Eu3+ decreases on the surface of carbon quantum dots as it gradually forms EuF3 with the different concentrations of fluoride ions added. From the de-convoluted spectra F1s of T-CQDs/Eu3+ containing F− (100 ppm) (Fig. 7b), a peak at 683.8 eV (metal-fluorides) is observed due to the formation of EuF3. The mechanism behind this process is also depicted in Fig. 10 . Thus, there is quenching of FL spectra (switch-off) of the T-CQDs on adding Eu3+ (100 ppm) on excitation at 320 nm (Fig. 11a). Again re-generation of the FL emission spectra (switch-on) is observed with added fluoride ions of different concentration. Fig. 10a describes the quenching of FL emission spectra of T-CQDs on addition of Eu3+ and the regain of FL spectra with addition of different concentration of fluoride ion. The type of change in the FL intensity with different fluoride concentrations can also be observed from the Fig. 11b and consequently the calibration curve is obtained (Fig. 11c), which signifies a substantial R-squared value for determination of any unknown fluoride solution within the ranges by using this promising T-CQDs/Eu3+ probe. However, the regression value could be made more significant by analysis of large number of standard fluoride solutions by this method.

In summary, a bio-compatible and simple ultrasonic-assisted chemical route for fabrication of CQDs from different carbonized biomass waste including sugarcane bagasse, taro peels, and garlic peels is demonstrated as compared with the other current toxic and tedious methods available. The as-fabricated CQDs exhibit good optical properties including high quantum yield, blue-fluorescence, and excellent stable fluorescence properties. The synthesized CQDs quenched with Eu3+ produces a good nanoprobe for the high selectivity detection of fluoride ions in water solutions. This work provided an innovative pathway for sensing of fluoride ions in water samples, and a new avenue of biomass waste management for its sustainable utilization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are highly grateful to the Director, CSIR-NEIST for his enthusiasm and support to this research work. Financial grants received from CSIR (MLP-1006 and OLP-2031) are duly acknowledged. The authors express their gratitude to Pallabi, Kabyashree, and Shristi Rupa for their assistance in raw sample collection and few preliminary routine analyses. Authors are thankful to the esteemed reviewers and editor for their constructive comments to improve the revision.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots from orange juice
J. Bionanosci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Kumar, K. Singh, V. Verma, H.S. Bhatti, Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots from orange juice, Journal of Bionanoscience 8 (2014) 274–279, doi.10.1166/jbns.2014.1236.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of carbon dots from kitchen waste: conversion of waste to value added product
J. Fluoresc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.L. Himaja, P.S. Karthik, B. Sreedhar, S.P. Singh, Synthesis of Carbon Dots from Kitchen Waste: Conversion of Waste to Value Added Product, J Fluoresc. 24 (2014) 1767–1773, doi.10.1007/s10895-014-1465-1.
